description:
You are a performance engineer about to start profiling a set of internal micro-services.  
Before any profiler can attach, your team’s convention requires that two specific
environment variables be set and recorded so that anyone can later confirm the
exact tuning values that were used.

Goal  
1. Create a dotenv file that the profiling launch scripts will later consume.  
2. Demonstrate, in the same terminal session, that the variables are truly available
   to any child process.  
3. Store that demonstration in a short, machine-verifiable log file.

Detailed requirements  

A. Dotenv file  
   • Exact absolute path:  
     /home/user/.config/profiling/.env  
   • It must contain **exactly** the following two lines and nothing else  
     (no leading or trailing spaces, no blank lines):  
       APP_MODE=profiling  
       PROFILER_PORT=6060  

B. Shell export  
   • Source (or otherwise apply) the dotenv file so that the current shell session
     has the two variables in its environment.

C. Verification run & log  
   • Still in the same shell session, execute a single command that prints **only**
     the two required variables in the form  
       KEY=value  
     one per line, in the same order as they appear in the dotenv file.  
   • Redirect that output to the log file  
     /home/user/profiling_env.log  

   When you open /home/user/profiling_env.log with a text viewer, it must look
   exactly—character by character—like this:

       APP_MODE=profiling
       PROFILER_PORT=6060

   (That is, two lines terminated by a UNIX newline character, no blank line at the
   end, and no extra whitespace.)

Important notes for the automated checker  

• The checker will confirm both the existence and exact byte-for-byte contents of  
  /home/user/.config/profiling/.env and /home/user/profiling_env.log.  
• The checker will also start a non-interactive shell, source the dotenv file, and
  expect both environment variables to be present with the correct values.  
• File and directory ownership must remain with the non-privileged user “user”.  
• You will not have root access; create any missing parent directories yourself.  
• Use standard POSIX shell tools only—no external language runtimes are available.  

Perform the work in the current terminal session. Once the log file contains the
exact text shown above, the task is complete.
truth:
Expected artifacts (exact contents follow, terminated by '\n' unless stated):

1. File: /home/user/.config/profiling/.env
   Contents (10 bytes total, two UNIX newlines):
   APP_MODE=profiling\n
   PROFILER_PORT=6060\n

2. File: /home/user/profiling_env.log
   Contents (same 10 bytes):
   APP_MODE=profiling\n
   PROFILER_PORT=6060\n

Environment expectations after sourcing /home/user/.config/profiling/.env:
   $APP_MODE == "profiling"
   $PROFILER_PORT == "6060"
